**Notes for the reader:**

- **Attic wedding:** Refers to a wedding from Attica, the region around ancient Athens.
- **Best man:** The male attendant who stands beside the groom during the ceremony.
- **Torches:** Lit torches were used in ancient Greek wedding processions to symbolize the transfer of the bride from her family to the groom's household, and to provide light for a night-time event.
- **Painted wood:** In ancient Greek architecture, columns were sometimes made of wood and painted white to imitate stone.

CuratorialText: This highly ornate and extremely well-preserved Roman marble sarcophagus came to the Metropolitan Museum from the collection of the Dukes of Beaufort and was formerly displayed in their country seat, Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England. An inscription on the unfinished back of the sarcophagus records that it was installed there in 1733. In contrast to the rough and unsightly back, the sides and front of the sarcophagus are decorated with forty human and animal figures carved in high relief. The central figure is that of the god Dionysos seated on a panther, but he is somewhat overshadowed by four larger standing figures who represent the four Seasons (from left to right, Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). The figures are unusual in that the Seasons are usually portrayed as women, but here they are shown as sturdy youths. Around these five central figures are placed other Bacchic figures and cultic objects, all carved at a smaller scale. On the rounded ends of the sarcophagus are two other groups of large figures, similarly intermingled with lesser ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is portrayed reclining on the ground; she is accompanied by a satyr and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure, probably to be identified with the personification of a river-god, reclines in front of two winged youths, perhaps representing two additional Seasons. The sarcophagus is an exquisite example of Roman funerary art, displaying all the virtuosity of the workshop where it was carved. The marble comes from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was probably shipped to Rome, where it was worked. Only a very wealthy and powerful person would have been able to commission and purchase such a sarcophagus, and it was probably made for a member of one of the old aristocratic families in Rome itself. The subjects - the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons - are unlikely, however, to have had any special significance for the deceased, particularly as it is clear that the design was copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another sarcophagus, now in the Hessisches Landesmuseum in Kassel, Germany, has the same composition of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons, although the treatment and carving of the figures is quite different. On the Badminton sarcophagus the figures are carved in high relief and so endow the crowded scene with multiple areas of light and shade, allowing the eye to wander effortlessly from one figure to another. One must also imagine that certain details were highlighted with color and even gilding, making the whole composition a visual tour de force. Very few Roman sarcophagi of this quality have survived. Although the Badminton sarcophagus lacks its lid, the fact that it was found in the early eighteenth century and soon thereafter installed in Badminton Hall means that it has been preserved almost intact and only a few of the minor extremities are now missing.

CuratorialText:
On the neck, obverse, battle of centaurs and Lapiths; reverse, youths and women
Around the body, Amazonomachy (battle between Greeks and Amazons). The ancient Greeks almost never depicted contemporary or historical events in art. Thus, while literary works of the fifth century B.C. make clear that the Greeks understood the magnitude of their victory in the Persian Wars, there was no concern among artists to illustrate major events or personalities. Instead, their preference was for grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern adversaries, notably Amazons. The Amazons were a mythical race of warrior women whose homeland lay far to the east and north. The most celebrated Amazonomachies in Athens during the first half of the fifth century were large-scale wall paintings that decorated the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico). Their influence was considerable and underlies the representation here and on the adjacent krater, 06.286.86.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.

Still lifes are carefully composed arrangements that may include shells, books, food, drink, tableware, skulls, dead animals, flowers, insects, and occasionally a human figure in the background. In these works, life is literally paused for a moment.

Soon, specific subgenres emerged. For example, Rachel Ruysch specialized in floral still lifes, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda focused on arrangements of food and drinking vessels. Another important type is the vanitas still life, which often includes a skull. This motif refers to the transience of life and the impermanence of material possessions.

CuratorialText: Orange above! With the coronation of King William I (1772-1843), the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time in 1815. This first king is the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I has canals and roads constructed in the Netherlands and invests in trade, shipping, and industry. However, with his sights firmly set on the economy, William I pays less attention to other affairs of state. Discontent in the Southern Netherlands grows. The population rises in revolt, and in 1831, the secession of Belgium becomes a reality. The king resists, but attempts to regain the territory come to nothing. After years of struggle, William I recognizes independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed, he abdicates the throne a year later, and his son, William II, takes over.

CuratorialText: The extensive costume collection of the Rijksmuseum, the oldest in the Netherlands, tells a wealth of stories. From women's and men's to children's clothing: these pieces reveal stories about the wearer and their taste, the methods of the makers, and local and global influences. The collection is a remarkable reflection of fashion developments throughout the centuries.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.

During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the outbreak of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau appeared in all fields of the arts.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first apprenticed with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden and subsequently continued his training with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt allowed himself to be unconstrained by rules and experimented freely with daring compositions, paint handling, and rendering of textures. He enhanced the drama in his depictions with strong light-dark contrasts. His early works are painted quite precisely; later, his brushstrokes became increasingly loose. He regularly received important commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, The Night Watch. Instead of a static portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment in which all the figures are depicted in action. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and approximately 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.

CuratorialText: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) is now world-famous, little is known about him. It is clear, however, that the native of Delft spent his days as an art dealer, father of a large family, and, naturally, a painter. His early work includes a Biblical and a mythological scene. Other early works reveal influences from the Utrecht Caravaggists. Vermeer primarily paints interiors and tronies (portrait-like studies of people). His most famous works are the intimate genre pieces, snapshots of everyday life: a lady reading a letter, a woman engaged in a household chore. All the scenes share a serene calm, which sometimes even feels somewhat mysterious. Vermeer is admired for his exceptional use of light, powerful colors, and incredible precision. During his lifetime, he rose to a certain fame and commanded high prices for his paintings. Afterward, Vermeer fell into obscurity and was not rediscovered until the mid-19th century.

CuratorialText: Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) is one of the most successful artists of her time. Her flower still lifes are very detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises: all almost indistinguishable from the real thing. Her work bears witness to a vast knowledge of nature. Ruysch acquires this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, teaches as a professor. She also studies his collection of prepared flowers and insects. She learns the tricks of the trade from still-life painter Willem van Aelst. In 1701, Ruysch becomes the first woman to join the Hague artists' society Pictura. From 1708 to 1716, she works as court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate, a particularly honorable position. He is by no means the only admirer of her work. Ruysch's flower still lifes are collected by enthusiasts far beyond the national borders.

CuratorialText: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into the artistic family of painter Johann Georg Schwartze and grew into a talented portrait painter, draftsman, and printmaker. Trained in her father's studio and after art education in Munich and Paris, she develops into a true talent. When her father dies prematurely, the 23-year-old Schwartze becomes the new breadwinner of the family. Her style is powerful, with rapid brushstrokes and large areas of color, which make her portraits full of character yet flattering. Schwartze's work is well received, and she becomes increasingly famous both at home and abroad. Around the year 1900, Schwartze is the most successful portraitist in the Netherlands, and she portrays members of the Royal Family. She works quickly and creates hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint during her career, of which the Rijksmuseum holds a number of artist portraits in its collection.

CuratorialText: Anyone walking through Amsterdam towards the end of the 19th century has a good chance of encountering George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). For days on end, the painter roams through his beloved Amsterdam with a sketchbook. He calls himself 'the painter of the people' and primarily draws and paints 'ordinary' city dwellers. Breitner also uses his camera to capture life. He subsequently applies his typical photographic style, in which figures partially fall outside the frame, to his paintings as well. This style, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudges, is not understood by everyone. Journalists are critical of his work, but enthusiasts love it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists of his time.

He then applied his characteristic photographic style—in which figures are partially cut off by the edge of the frame—to his paintings as well. This style, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudged areas, was not understood by everyone. Journalists were critical of his work, but enthusiasts loved it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists of his time.

CuratorialText: He drew extensively and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a cousin by marriage and a renowned painter. In his early years, Van Gogh primarily drew and painted many peasant scenes in dark colors. After a brief period of study in Antwerp, Van Gogh left for Paris, where he was influenced by Impressionism and Pointillism. He developed a style characterized by short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors applied side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny Southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues, for which he was hospitalized. However, this did not prevent him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death, in 1890, that his work reached the general public and Van Gogh became world-famous.

CuratorialText: Born into a Utrecht family of silversmiths, the career path of Adam van Vianen (1568/69-1627) was determined early on. Around 1590, Van Vianen must have completed his master's examination, as he worked independently from that moment on and joined the blacksmiths' guild. His imaginative objects tell a story through lobe-like ornaments and forms. In 1614, Van Vianen received a commission from the Amsterdam guild to create a jug in memory of his own brother Paulus, who was also a silversmith and had passed away shortly before. The jug became a tribute to his brother's work and creativity. In this jug, the ornament plays the leading role for the first time.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.

CuratorialText: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. Here, people live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. But however remote the village may be, the influence of the modern world is clearly noticeable. For instance, this community also possesses mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series that Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life in abstract compositions. Largely in black and white, but with the colorful splashes characteristic of Sassen's earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series was added to the collection of the Rijksmuseum as a modern addition to the collection of photographs of Suriname, which until then had been primarily historical in character.

CuratorialText: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Revolt and was revered as the 'father of the fatherland'. At the age of 11, William inherited the French principality of Orange and received the title 'Prince of Orange'. He led a turbulent life with an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip strived for an empire with central authority and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, on the other hand, stood for freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This struggle cost him his life on July 10, 1584, from a gunshot.

This struggle ultimately cost him his life: on July 10, 1584, he was killed by a gunshot.

CuratorialText: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? Little is known about her. However, it is clear that around the year 1906, she modeled for Simon Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. This is evident from photographs Maris took of the young lady in his studio. Documents have been preserved in Simon Maris's archive in which he refers to her as 'Isabella'. Some other paintings Maris made of the girl were also given this name. Nevertheless, it is not certain that this young lady was really named 'Isabella'. It is possible that Maris invented this name for her. Or perhaps she used a pseudonym herself, as models often did at that time.

CuratorialText: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) played a crucial role in increasing the prestige of the House of Orange and the international positioning of the Netherlands. She serves as a sounding board and advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he falls ill, she assumes some of his duties and charts a course that heralds the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son passes away, marking the beginning of the first stadtholderless era. To safeguard the future of the House of Orange, Van Solms exerts national and international influence. She uses art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects competes with other European courts. In doing so, she firmly establishes the Netherlands, with the House of Orange at its head, as a European center of art and trade.

Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects rivaled those of other European courts. In this way, she firmly established the Netherlands—with the House of Orange at its center—as a European hub for art and trade.

CuratorialText: Between 1650 and 1672, there was no Stadtholder. The most powerful citizens governed the country. As Grand Pensionary of Holland, Johan de Witt rose to become the most influential politician. He cunningly plays England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other. Nevertheless, these countries, together with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attack the Netherlands in 1672. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversees the military activities as a deputy of the States General. As the foreign troops gain ground, the call for the return of the Stadtholder grows ever louder. Cornelis is arrested on suspicion of conspiracy against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visits him in prison, an incited mob drags the brothers out and kills them. Long after their deaths, both are remembered as champions of 'true freedom'.

CuratorialText: At the beginning of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together, including the future Emperor Napoleon III, but it was an unhappy marriage. Queen Hortense was often present at festivities and official occasions and, partly due to her visits to local businesses, became increasingly popular with the people. That was until her husband, displeased with her popularity, forced her into the background. After Napoleon's defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive Arenenberg estate, De Beauharnais, herself a talented musician and composer, organized cultural gatherings that were talked about for a long time afterwards.

CuratorialText: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after a construction period of nearly ten years. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the exterior of the Rijksmuseum, Cuypers depicted himself, just as the master builders did in the decoration of cathedrals during the Middle Ages. Over the years, many original decorations in the museum have disappeared. During the last renovation, however, Cuypers' work was restored to its former glory. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers' only showpiece. In 1889, 'his' Central Station in Amsterdam was unveiled. In addition to these famous projects, Cuypers focused primarily on churches and monuments.

CuratorialText: Willem Drees (1886-1988) made a career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which would later become the PvdA. The politician climbed a long ladder of various positions: municipal councilor, alderman, Member of Parliament. In the first cabinet elected after the war, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs. With his Drees Emergency Act, a provision for fixed benefits, he helped the elderly out of financial distress. Drees was popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. It was his task to effectively handle the reconstruction of the Netherlands. Drees proved to be the right man in the right place. With a sense of solidarity, the Netherlands was rebuilt. Under Drees's leadership, until 1958, the foundation of the modern Dutch welfare state was laid. To this day, the statesman is appreciated for his efforts for the Netherlands.

He introduced the Drees Emergency Act, a law that provided fixed financial benefits to help elderly people escape poverty. Drees was popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. His main task was to manage the reconstruction of the Netherlands effectively. He proved to be the right person for this role. With a strong sense of collective responsibility, the country was rebuilt. Under Drees’s leadership, which lasted until 1958, the foundations of the modern Dutch welfare state were established. Even today, this statesman is respected for his contributions to the Netherlands.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 16th century, there was a preference in Italy for Mannerism, a style full of complex compositions and figures in intricate, twisted poses. Around 1600, the Italian Baroque artist Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio broke radically with this art form. His figures are actually very realistic. Wrinkled faces, unkempt hair, dirty feet: gods and saints are depicted as very ordinary people. Caravaggio has a preference for strong light-dark contrasts, dramatic effects, and compositions with a clear structure. His method spreads rapidly throughout Europe. Especially in Utrecht, around 1620, many followers, known as Caravaggists, can be found. Artists such as Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen travel to Rome to study Caravaggio's style. And although he never visited Italy, Rembrandt was also inspired by Caravaggio's lighting effects.

**Adapted Text:**

At the end of the 16th century, Mannerism was the preferred style in Italy. This style is characterized by complex compositions and figures in intricate, twisted poses. Around 1600, the Italian Baroque artist Caravaggio broke radically with this tradition. His figures are highly realistic. He shows gods and saints with wrinkled faces, messy hair, and dirty feet—like ordinary people. Caravaggio favored strong contrasts between light and dark, dramatic effects, and clear, structured compositions. His method spread quickly across Europe. Around 1620, many followers—called Caravaggists—were active, especially in Utrecht. Artists such as Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen traveled to Rome to study Caravaggio’s style. Although Rembrandt never visited Italy, he was also inspired by Caravaggio’s use of light and shadow.

CuratorialText: The 15th and 16th centuries are broadly regarded as the period of the Renaissance, a term that literally means 'rebirth'. In Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences flourish due to a new appreciation of the classical antiquity of the Romans and Greeks. Artists and architects seek ideal proportions. They study Roman architecture and adopt formal languages ​​such as round arches, columns, and pediments. The visual arts increasingly focus on a realistic representation of nature and man, using perspective and anatomical studies. Alongside religious themes, secular themes and mythological representations become popular. The Renaissance spreads from Italy throughout Europe, thanks to the rise of printmaking and printing in the mid-15th century.

The 15th and 16th centuries are widely considered the Renaissance period—a term that means "rebirth." In Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences flourished due to a renewed appreciation of classical antiquity, specifically the cultures of ancient Rome and Greece. Artists and architects sought ideal proportions. They studied Roman architecture and adopted formal elements such as round arches, columns, and pediments. In the visual arts, there was an increasing focus on realistic representations of nature and the human figure, achieved through the use of perspective and anatomical studies. Alongside religious subjects, secular themes and mythological narratives became popular. The Renaissance spread from Italy throughout Europe, largely due to the rise of printmaking and the printing press in the mid-15th century.

CuratorialText: The period between the First and Second World Wars is dominated by Art Deco, an international art movement in which tradition and industrial progress go hand in hand. Emerging from Art Nouveau, Art Deco also draws inspiration from stylized plant and floral motifs as well as linear, geometric designs. Additionally, artists look to folk art and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America, a source of inspiration that is further fueled by archaeological finds. The term Art Deco is derived from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes.
persona2_adapted: The period between the First and Second World Wars is defined by Art Deco, an international art movement that combines tradition with industrial progress. It developed from Art Nouveau and also takes inspiration from stylized plant and floral patterns, as well as linear and geometric designs. In addition, artists look to folk art and artistic traditions from Africa, East Asia, and Central America—a source of inspiration further encouraged by archaeological discoveries. The name "Art Deco" comes from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, officially called the *Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes*.

CuratorialText: From around 1150, increasingly tall churches rose in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame as the most famous example. It is seen as the beginning of Gothic architecture, a style that spread rapidly throughout the rest of Europe and would continue until shortly after 1500. Church walls became airier thanks to tall pointed arch windows fitted with colorful stained glass; the vaults rested on slender columns and were strikingly ribbed, and on the exterior, the masonry was supported by slender flying buttresses with pinnacles. Under the influence of the Gothic style, sculptors also gave their human, animal, and plant figures a stylized appearance with elegant poses, calligraphic drapery, and graceful gestures. Meanwhile, the forms of Gothic architecture also seeped into other art forms, for example, painting, illuminated manuscripts, metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum possesses numerous ecclesiastical objects in this style, such as painted altars, silver monstrances, wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.
persona2_adapted:
From around 1150, increasingly tall churches began to appear in and around Paris. The most famous example is Notre-Dame, which is considered the starting point of Gothic architecture. This style spread quickly across Europe and continued until shortly after 1500.

Church walls became lighter and more open, thanks to tall pointed arch windows filled with colorful stained glass. The vaults rested on slender columns and had visible ribs. On the outside, the stone structure was supported by thin flying buttresses with pinnacles.

Under the influence of Gothic style, sculptors also gave their figures—whether human, animal, or plant—a stylized look. These figures have elegant poses, flowing drapery, and graceful gestures. The forms of Gothic architecture also entered other art forms, such as painting, illuminated manuscripts, metalwork, and furniture.

The Rijksmuseum has many religious objects in this style, including painted altars, silver monstrances, wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.

CuratorialText: The term Impressionism, which means snapshot, was initially used in the early 1870s as a negative label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot depict reality as seen through the eyes of the artist. The French Impressionists are inspired by the realistic style of the French Barbizon School and the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They work with bright colors and short, loose brushstrokes. They choose their themes from modern life. Due to the sketchy approach, it sometimes appears as if their paintings were created quickly and spontaneously. This often leads critics to say that the artworks are unfinished. From the late 1880s onwards, appreciation for French Impressionism also grows in the Netherlands.

The term *Impressionism*—which literally means "snapshot"—was first used in the early 1870s as a derogatory label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Artists such as Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot aimed to depict reality as seen through the artist's own eyes. The French Impressionists were inspired by the realistic approach of the French Barbizon School, as well as by the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They worked with bright colors and employed short, loose brushstrokes. Their subject matter was drawn from modern life. Because of this sketch-like technique, their paintings often appear to have been made quickly and spontaneously. This led many critics to claim that the works looked unfinished. From the late 1880s onward, appreciation for French Impressionism also began to grow in the Netherlands.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona2_adapted: At the end of the 19th century, a new artistic style emerged. It was a reaction by artists who rejected the dominant historicism of the time—a tendency to look back to the styles of earlier centuries. This new style, appropriately called Art Nouveau, is defined by natural forms such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. Its key features include flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With major centers in Paris, Brussels, and Munich, Art Nouveau developed into an international movement across Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from around 1890 until the outbreak of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau appeared in all areas of the arts.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt is not only a successful painter, but also exceptionally talented as a draftsman and etcher. The Rijksmuseum collection contains approximately 1,300 prints by his hand, including sometimes multiple copies and different states of a single print. The subjects of his etchings range from biblical and mythological scenes to everyday scenes, landscapes, and (self-)portraits. Rembrandt's development in technique, style, and artistic approach can be clearly seen in these works. His loose drawing style and the high-contrast rendering of light and shadow are unique. The choice to depict the most emotional moments is also remarkable for his time. It makes his work human and relatable. Rembrandt has thus had a major influence on artists of his own time and for a long time thereafter.

The Rijksmuseum holds around 1,300 prints made by his hand, including multiple copies and different states of the same print. His etchings cover a wide range of subjects, from biblical and mythological scenes to everyday life, landscapes, and (self-)portraits. These works clearly show Rembrandt’s evolution in technique, style, and artistic approach. His loose drawing style and the strong contrast between light and shadow are distinctive. For his time, it was also remarkable that he chose to depict the most emotionally charged moments. This makes his work feel human and relatable. As a result, Rembrandt had a major influence on artists of his own era and for a long time afterward.

CuratorialText: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony, alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger succeeded in becoming the first in Europe to produce porcelain in 1708. Not long afterwards, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. The factory played an important role in the dream of August the Strong: a Japanese Palace, in which he wanted to exhibit his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the end of the 16th century, porcelain, also known as 'white gold', had been imported from Asia and collected by the wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that porcelain was now also being produced in Europe is one of the most important moments in the history of the European applied arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including pieces from the factory's early production from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.

Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony, the alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger became the first person in Europe to successfully produce porcelain in 1708. Shortly after, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. This factory was central to Augustus the Strong’s vision for a Japanese Palace, where he intended to display his collection of both Asian and Meissen porcelain.

Since the late 16th century, porcelain—often referred to as "white gold"—had been imported from Asia and collected by wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that Europe could now produce its own porcelain marks one of the most significant moments in the history of European applied arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including early pieces from the factory, originally part of the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The largest part of this is Chinese (approx. 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (approx. 600), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. After all, the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602). From the 17th century onwards, thin-fired porcelain with Chinese blue decorations became immensely popular. Consequently, Chinese and Japanese porcelain play an important role in Dutch interiors. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been housed in the Rijksmuseum, comprising pieces that are part of art production in Asia: tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.
persona2_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has collected Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The collection consists mainly of Chinese pieces (around 2,300) and Japanese pieces (around 600), with an additional 50 works from other Asian countries. This focus is historically logical: the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the establishment of the Dutch East India Company (VOC) in 1602. From the 17th century onward, thin, high-fired porcelain decorated with blue Chinese motifs became extremely popular. As a result, Chinese and Japanese porcelain became important elements in Dutch interiors.

Since 1952, the Rijksmuseum has also housed the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK). This collection includes objects that represent different aspects of Asian artistic production: Japanese tea ceramics, Chinese Song dynasty bowls and Tang dynasty funerary sculptures, as well as ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

CuratorialText: From 1610 onwards, around one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands annually. To withstand the Chinese competition, Dutch potters began to refine their products. The earthenware becomes thinner, whiter, and shinier. Blue paintings are applied to a base of white tin glaze, a color scheme strongly associated with Asian porcelain. This earthenware is called 'plateel' or 'faience', but in the 17th and 18th centuries it acquires the name 'Hollants porceleyn'. From 1650 onwards, Delft becomes the center of earthenware production, hence the eventual name 'Delft earthenware'. During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain comes to a virtual standstill due to the civil war in China (1644-1684). This gives Delft earthenware the opportunity to flourish and gain popularity among the European elite.
persona2_adapted: From 1610 onward, the Netherlands imported approximately 100,000 pieces of Chinese porcelain each year. In response to this competition, Dutch potters began to improve the quality of their own ceramics. The earthenware became thinner, whiter, and more lustrous. Blue decoration was applied over a white tin glaze—a color combination strongly linked to Asian porcelain. This type of earthenware was known as *plateel* or *faience*, but during the 17th and 18th centuries, it came to be called *Hollants porceleyn* ("Dutch porcelain"). After 1650, Delft emerged as the main center of production, which eventually led to the name "Delft earthenware." During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain nearly stopped due to the civil war in China (1644–1684). This interruption allowed Delft earthenware to thrive and gain popularity among Europe’s elite.

CuratorialText: The bronze collection of the Rijksmuseum contains a collection of small sculptures from the Renaissance, primarily from Italy. Bronze casting in the style of classical antiquity also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. In the 16th and 17th centuries, these figurines found their way into the studies of humanist scholars, art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and Kunstkammern of princes and nobility.

CuratorialText: Originally, artists in seascapes primarily captured historical events at sea, so that posterity could relive the heroic deeds. 'War correspondent' and artist Willem van de Velde I created eyewitness paintings of famous Dutch naval battles. His son Willem was a master at capturing calm or, conversely, stormy seas. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen opted for a dramatized depiction of ships in distress. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the effect of light and cloudy skies played a major role, and they paid more attention to the harsh life of fishermen.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.

The Rijksmuseum holds an extensive collection of portraits, spanning from the late Middle Ages to the present day. Beyond their artistic merit, these more than 1,700 works give a face to the past. They show us what people looked like: the clothing and jewelry they wore, their hairstyles, and sometimes the interiors of their time.

CuratorialText: Genre paintings are paintings depicting scenes from everyday life. They are lively scenes that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, and markets. They capture people in their natural environment: children romping, a family huddled together in the kitchen, or a merchant hawking his wares. The first genre paintings emerged in 16th-century Flemish painting, but in the Netherlands, genre art did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous. They often concealed a mischievous content or, conversely, a moral message in their work. The term 'genre paintings' did not emerge until the 19th century, when the popularity of genre art reached a peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.

CuratorialText: Water is crucial for the development of the Netherlands as a trading nation. The economy grows thanks to the raw materials that enter via rivers and the world's oceans. Consider bulk goods such as grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Using these raw materials, the Dutch produce items such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp is the largest port city of the Netherlands. After the (re)conquest by the Spaniards during the Dutch Revolt, Holland, and especially Amsterdam, takes over the role as a staple market. International trade leads to the emergence of a financial market, significant employment, and strong urban growth.

What is money actually worth? Why does a small metal coin, worth only a few euro cents as a material, suddenly have a much higher value simply because it is stamped with the number '2 euro'? The person or institution that has the right to mint coins decides three things: the design, the amount of precious metal used, and the official value.

Until well into the 18th century, large groups of ships, called "silver fleets," sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money then traveled to other parts of the world, for example to Asia, through the Dutch East India Company (VOC).

CuratorialText: William of Orange is regarded as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because of his leading role in the struggle for independence. Nevertheless, it seems to be the end for the House of Orange at the end of the 18th century: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is proclaimed. From that moment on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always sat on the throne.

CuratorialText: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell something about the significance of the object. Provenance research reveals stories of pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not looted, their provenance provides insight into their history. The Rijksmuseum collection primarily tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Research into the collection can therefore not possibly provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. After all, many everyday objects did not end up in museums or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums are still the silent witnesses of this today.

The transport of the products to Europe and their further processing also generates revenue. In this way, colonial trade stimulates the national economy.

CuratorialText: Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to the present day, in every shape and size and encompassing a wide range of materials. This expanding online catalog presents a comprehensive overview of the latest scholarly insights into the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly diverse collection of European sculpture. This online catalog includes every work of European sculpture in the Rijksmuseum's art collection. The most recently published catalog of sculpture dates from 1973. Prompting a thorough update of this valuable, but by now outdated reference work was the ever-growing body of art historical research and the long list of acquisitions made in recent decades.   The Rijksmuseum (and its precursors: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began acquiring sculpture on a systematic basis only in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Additions to the collection prior to this time are few in number. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase ofArtus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust portrait of Andries de Graeff in 1817. Initially, the museum's sculpture acquisition policy was primarily oriented to works of national interest. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to shift, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and compiler of the 1973 catalogue), who sought to actively acquire international works. As a result, sculptures of non-Netherlandish origin today make up approximately half of the collection. This international scope received an additional impulse when, in 1952, the Dutch government charged the Rijksmuseum with the care of Italian and German works formerly in the possession of Fritz Mannheimer and recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This enhancement of the collection has since served to stimulate the museum's policy of acquiring international works.

CuratorialText:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.
Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') constitute one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image and because it presents a pictorial puzzle, which provides the viewer with a particular insight into the intellectual and literary world of the late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). **Adapted Text:**

The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books, and drawings. Among the nearly 4,000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published *surimono*. This online catalog presents 203 *surimono* donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943–2011). The catalog entries are based on Matthi Forrer’s 2013 collection catalog *Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam*.

**Poetry and Image Combined**

*Surimono* (literally "printed things") represent one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre is fascinating because it combines poetry and image, and because it presents a visual puzzle. This puzzle offers the viewer a special insight into the intellectual and literary world of late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today’s Tokyo). Major artists—such as Katsushika Hokusai, Utagawa Kunisada, Toyota Hokkei, and Yashima Gakutei, among others—created images to accompany the poetic works of poetry club members.

---

**Notes on adaptation:**

- **"Exclusive, privately published"** is kept as is, since these are clear, art-historical terms.
- **"Pictorial puzzle"** replaces "pictorial puzzle" with "visual puzzle" for clarity, though the original is also acceptable.
- **"Poetic exploits"** is changed to "poetic works" to avoid the idiomatic and slightly informal tone of "exploits."
- **"To name but a few"** is replaced with "among others," which is more direct and commonly understood.
- **"Based on"** replaces "taken from" for precision and formality.
- **"Nearly"** replaces "almost" as a more formal equivalent.
- **"Special insight"** replaces "particular insight" for naturalness, though "particular" is also fine.

CuratorialText: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.

---

**Adapted Text:**

*Fishing for Souls* is an allegorical painting that represents the religious tensions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. The scene takes place in a river landscape, with large groups of people gathered on both banks. Several rowboats are on the water, and a rainbow appears on the horizon.

On the left side, we see the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry; Frederick V of the Palatinate; James I, King of England; and the young Louis XIII, King of France, together with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground, Protestant fishermen are at work. Their nets are marked with the words *Fides* (Faith), *Spes* (Hope), and *Charitas* (Charity).

On the right side, the Catholics and their supporters are shown: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope, who is carried by cardinals. In the smaller Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the direction of a bishop.

---

**Notes for the reader:**

- *Allegory*: A story or image in which characters or events represent abstract ideas or moral qualities.
- *Twelve Years' Truce*: A temporary peace (1609–1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain during the Eighty Years' War.
- *Fides, Spes, Charitas*: Latin for Faith, Hope, and Charity—the three theological virtues in Christian tradition.
- *Princes of Orange*: Members of the Dutch House of Orange-Nassau, key leaders in the Dutch struggle for independence.
- *Frederick V of the Palatinate*: A German prince and a key Protestant figure in the early 17th century.
- *Archdukes Albert and Isabella*: The Habsburg rulers of the Spanish Netherlands.
- *General Ambrosio Spinola*: A Spanish-Italian military commander for Spain.
- *Cardinals*: High-ranking officials in the Catholic Church.

CuratorialText: The word Sufi often conjures up images of Turkish whirling dervishes, turning gracefully round themselves in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, not much is known of the beliefs and practices in which such ceremonies are rooted, or about the communities that embrace them.
persona2_adapted: The term *Sufi* frequently brings to mind images of Turkish whirling dervishes, who spin elegantly during a mystical ritual. However, beyond the realm of Islamic mysticism, there is limited understanding of the beliefs and practices that form the foundation of such ceremonies, or of the communities that observe them.

CuratorialText: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts imbued with Sufi connotations. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and regionality. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and asceticism, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.
persona2_adapted:
This exhibition explores Sufi life and art through selected objects representing Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. Sufism is a mystical movement that emerged in the Islamic world in the 8th century AD. It is understood as a path toward a personal experience of God.

The display includes Islamic artefacts from the British Museum that carry Sufi meanings. Some objects, like alms bowls, are clearly associated with dervishes. Others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings, and wall tiles.

Sufis come from diverse social backgrounds, crossing boundaries of class, ethnicity, and region. The exhibition examines themes such as mystical love, poverty, and asceticism, as well as the influence of Sufism on the wider societies in which it exists.

CuratorialText: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.

**Adapted Text:**

**Hawaiʻi: A Kingdom Crossing Oceans** is a visually rich exhibition that celebrates the artistic traditions and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together a remarkable range of objects: feathered cloaks once worn by chiefs, finely carved representations of deities, powerful weapons made with shark teeth, and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.

In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across the ocean on a journey that would become a turning point in the history of their kingdom. The exhibition marks more than 200 years since this royal visit—a visit that ended in tragedy—and explores the complex and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

Based on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners, and scholars, the exhibition places indigenous knowledge at the center. It brings new understanding to exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Developed together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the show presents rarely seen international loans alongside the British Museum’s own remarkable collection—one of the largest outside of Hawaiʻi.

---

- *Knowledge-bearers* → people who hold and pass on traditional cultural knowledge
- *Shining new light* → offering a new or deeper understanding

CuratorialText: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This exhibition presents prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist, and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006). The works explore the theme of revolution and its connection to contemporary society.

Finlay first gained recognition in the late 1950s for his poetry. In the 1960s, he expanded his practice to include prints and three-dimensional works made from stone, metal, neon, and glass. He created these pieces in collaboration with various skilled artisans. Finlay was also a dedicated gardener. He developed a conceptual garden at his remote home near the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh. Many of his artworks were integrated into this garden.

He named his garden *Little Sparta*, after the ancient Greek city that was a militaristic rival to Athens. This name reflects Finlay’s deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and ruptures, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's engagement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively on Finlay and who corresponded with the artist for many years.

CuratorialText: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.
persona2_adapted: In a career spanning the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) lived through some of the most dramatic events in French history. He was a student of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805) and first gained recognition by imitating the genre scenes that made Greuze famous at the Salons of the 1760s. Wille’s work showed a preference for delicacy, which matched the tastes of the ancien régime (the old social and political system in France before the Revolution).

CuratorialText: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.

Here is the adapted version of the text, tailored for a non-native English speaker with deep art knowledge.

---

**Adapted Text:**

Nevertheless, Wille as a person embraced the Revolution. He even joined the republican army and occupied himself with documenting the important figures of his time. However, in the end, the Revolution turned out to be more of a difficulty than an opportunity. In his younger years, Wille had worked in various styles, but later in life, he found it hard to adjust to the more restrained taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had been living in poverty for a long time.

During his life and after his death, Wille was better known for his drawings than for his paintings. The British Museum has been collecting his work since the 19th century, and its collection recently doubled in size thanks to a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift includes some of Wille’s finest finished drawings. One of the most powerful among them is a portrait that claims to show Charlotte Corday, the so-called "Angel of Assassination." It was made in her prison cell just days or even hours before her execution.

This exhibition, celebrating Clark’s bequest, presents Wille’s world through his drawings and those of his contemporaries. It reveals him as a highly skilled draughtsman, whose works offer a fascinating view into French society before, during, and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.

**Adapted Text**

Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later traveled to West Africa. In the 1880s, it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene (the king of the Asante people) in Kumasi, in present-day Ghana. During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896, British forces looted the ewer, and it was later purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and visible in the same photograph, was given to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This exhibition reunites these two vessels.

A major new research project on the Asante Ewer—one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting—is helping to further trace its journey to Ghana, as well as its status and meaning in West Africa. Its placement at the center of the palace courtyard, inside what appears to be a shrine and embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it held significant cultural or ritual value.

Remarkably, the ewer survived the long journey from Europe to West Africa and back. It has had very different lives: first as a luxury English jug, then as a sacred West African vessel, and finally as colonial loot taken by British troops.

CuratorialText: This display forms part of a three-year research project, The age of copper, ivory and gold, supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation, exploring the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. It is the result of close collaboration with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, York Army Museum and many others across Ghana, the US and the UK.

CuratorialText: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic innovation. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.
persona2_adapted: In the 15th and 16th centuries, the Low Countries were a major center of artistic innovation. During this transformative period, Netherlandish drawings changed from being merely preparatory sketches into independent works of art.

CuratorialText: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.
persona2_adapted:
Featuring approximately 120 works by artists such as Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder, and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition traces the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional range and quality of the British Museum’s collection offer a rare opportunity to present a comprehensive overview of drawing from this region.

Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare because they were originally created as functional objects within the workshop and were not typically preserved. This contrasts with Italy and the German-speaking regions, where a growing interest in collecting drawings during the 1500s helped ensure the survival of many more works from that period. Drawings played a central role in the design and production of artworks in various media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass, and prints. The surviving examples provide valuable insight into the creativity and collaborative practices of Netherlandish artists.

CuratorialText: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.
persona2_adapted: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, this exhibition presents new discoveries made by combining curatorial and conservation knowledge with scientific analysis. For example, new research into drawings previously linked only to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle shows that they actually come directly from his workshop. These drawings were likely made by his students as part of their training.

CuratorialText: Be among the first to view designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.

CuratorialText: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.

This memorial in St James' Park will feature a 10-foot bronze statue of Queen Elizabeth II as a young monarch, placed on a 14-foot plinth. It is designed to honor the late Queen and her lifelong dedication to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028.

The statue will be sculpted in bronze by the well-known artist Martin Jennings. It will depict Her Majesty early in her reign, wearing the robes of the Order of the Garter. The design takes inspiration from a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni.

---

**Notes for the reader:**
- *Plinth*: the base or pedestal on which a statue stands.
- *Order of the Garter*: the most senior and oldest British order of knighthood, founded in 1348.
- *Pietro Annigoni*: an Italian painter (1910–1988) known for his realistic portraits, including two official portraits of Queen Elizabeth II.

CuratorialText: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.

CuratorialText: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for relaxation and reflection. CuratorialText:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text**

This tour examines how people in ancient Egypt lived, died, and envisioned the afterlife. It draws on a wide range of funerary objects—including human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis, tomb models, wall paintings, and papyri—to explore what tombs, burial goods, and preserved bodies reveal about both daily life and beliefs about the world beyond death.

The tour covers three main themes: the preparation of the body for burial (including mummification), the arrangement of the tomb to ensure an eternal and peaceful existence, and the use of magical practices to guide and protect the deceased on their dangerous journey to the afterlife. It also demonstrates how funerary remains can provide insights into the lives of the living.

Please note that this tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum is committed to handling these materials with care, respect, and dignity. For more information, please consult our principles regarding the holding, display, and study of human remains.

CuratorialText:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.

Among these are the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. This special guided tour will explain the significance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs, and other contexts.

CuratorialText: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.

Its mission is to present the story of human cultural achievement through a series of collections. This room highlights several collectors who, in different ways, have influenced the Museum over the past four centuries, as well as individuals and organizations that continue to shape its future. The adjacent galleries also examine various aspects of collecting. Room 1, titled "Enlightenment," explains how objects and knowledge were gathered and classified during the Museum’s early years. Room 2a, "The Waddesdon Bequest," displays a collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces bequeathed to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP upon his death in 1898.

CuratorialText: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.
persona2_adapted: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, ensuring that the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for future generations.
