This pendant is unlike any other object in the British Museum’s collection or elsewhere in the UK. The highly skilled jewel features enamelled motifs and is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a clasp shaped like a hand emerging from a cloud. Few surviving objects tell the story of Henry’s life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a narrative largely erased after the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union, the longest of all Henry’s marriages, lasted 24 years and profoundly shaped the first three decades of his reign.

CuratorialText: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium. In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus revolutionised the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and classified. His pupil, Daniel Solander, was a curator in the Museum and applied the system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history specimens Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage.  Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, as well as a number of fossils. By 1880 there were so many natural history specimens that they needed a museum of their own – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.

**Adapted Text:**

In the 18th century, museum collections were divided into two categories: "Natural and Artificial Rarities." The first group included objects found in nature, while the second consisted of objects made by humans. In the early museum, only a few rooms contained manmade objects. However, many display cases were filled with natural specimens from Sloane’s collection, as well as his library, which included 265 volumes of his herbarium.

In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus changed the way plants, animals, and other natural objects were named and classified. His student, Daniel Solander, worked as a curator in the museum. He applied Linnaeus’s system to Sloane’s collections and also to the natural history specimens he himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks during the first voyage of Captain Cook. Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, along with a number of fossils.

By 1880, the museum had so many natural history specimens that they needed a separate building. This led to the creation of the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.

**Adapted Text:**

Antiquaries collected objects from the past—such as books, manuscripts, drawings, and artefacts—and traveled across the country to study ruins. Their goal was to learn more about Britain’s Roman past and its earlier history. It was relatively easy to study the medieval period because many objects from that time had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books, and buildings.

However, antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and even older sites—like Stonehenge—using more scientific and systematic methods. This allowed them to learn more about the earliest inhabitants of Britain. This new knowledge, combined with the emerging study of rock strata (layers of rock), led archaeologists to question the widely accepted date of the world’s creation: 4004 BC, a date calculated from readings of the Bible.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.
persona2_adapted: In 1824, the British Museum acquired Charles Townley’s renowned collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, which he had gathered during his Grand Tours in Italy. These works joined the existing collections of prints, drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases, and other classical antiquities from other connoisseurs, such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These individuals studied one another’s collections to understand what they saw as the “progress” of art—from what they considered “primitive” beginnings in early civilizations to what they regarded as the highest artistic achievement: the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This ideal became the standard for evaluating all art and, in late 18th-century Britain, inspired a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
persona2_adapted: During the 18th century, all collected items had to be classified and organized to make them easier to understand and use. King George III’s library was given to the nation by his son, George IV, and was originally placed in what is now the Enlightenment Gallery (the books themselves are now kept at the British Library). The collection of coins and medals was among the first parts of the library to arrive at the Museum; they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history to life. George III was also very interested in the navy, navigation, and scientific discoveries, and he collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton organized his vases, found in the area around Naples, according to painting styles, trying to establish a chronological order for them. He later published these vases in beautifully illustrated books. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious “artificial” (man-made) objects from around the world, including items collected from enslaved people in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways—by type, material, date, or purpose.

CuratorialText: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
persona2_adapted: The Enlightenment sparked an intense curiosity to understand the mysterious symbols of Egyptian hieroglyphs, the cuneiform writing found in biblical lands, and the early forms of Sanskrit in India. In the 19th century, scholars successfully "decoded" these scripts, unlocking thousands of years of history. Meanwhile, antiquarians deciphered runes and other ancient writing systems, revealing more about our own past. During this period, a large number of antiquities—acquired from collectors, diplomats, and through excavations—entered the British Museum.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.
persona2_adapted: In Christian tradition, suspicion of superstition, idols, rituals, and magic often resulted in a lack of knowledge about other religions. During the Enlightenment, scholars tried to correct this by studying both ancient and contemporary religions they came across. They investigated rituals and cults in various cultures, gathered religious objects, organized deities into groups, and analyzed their characteristics and roles. Their goal was to identify commonalities that could help explain the origins of religions around the world. Much of this research occurred within the framework of colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.
persona2_adapted:
In this period of colonialism and expanding global trade, travelers, merchants, scientists, explorers, and diplomats assembled collections of ethnographic objects. These included both ceremonial and everyday items, which were considered representative of the customs and cultures of peoples around the world. They collected artifacts and published illustrated accounts describing the lives and cultures of these peoples, always from a European perspective.

Alongside the collection of Sir Hans Sloane, the British Museum also holds objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti, and other Pacific islands. A significant number of these objects were acquired through employees of the East India Company and the Royal Africa Company. The wealth of these companies was made possible by the use of enslaved labor and the exploitation of people and natural resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.

**Adapted Text:**

During his rule, from 336 to 323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states and the Persian Empire. He also founded more than 70 cities, creating an empire that extended across three continents and covered about two million square miles.

The Hellenistic period begins with the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC and ends with the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After Alexander’s death, his empire was divided into kingdoms ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored large architectural projects and were important supporters of the arts and culture.

Objects displayed in Room 22 include a large carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in southwestern Turkey, and Greek bronzes. Smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters, coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines), and gem cutters.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.

These practitioners are *tohunga* (experts) who are building and continuing to develop Pacific cultural knowledge. During the show, the collective will sing, chant, and offer prayers to them with *Aroha* (love) from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText: One half of the gallery presents the histories of China from 5000 BC to the present: from iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain to delicate handscrolls, from magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern works of art. The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.

CuratorialText: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.

Notable highlights include seals from the Indus Valley civilization, exceptional South Indian sculptures of Shiva, and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. You will also find refined paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors, displayed alongside 20th-century works, including those by the Nobel Prize-winning artist Rabindranath Tagore.

CuratorialText: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.

**Adapted Text**

China is one of the world’s oldest civilizations. Today, it covers a vast area, about the size of Europe, and is home to one-quarter of the world’s population. From very early times, China developed a highly distinctive culture, producing beautifully crafted objects on a large, industrial scale.

Room 33 explores 7,000 years of Chinese history through themes such as writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, and international trade. The room also examines the history of South Asia, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Key themes here include trade, exchange, migration, and political power.

South Asia includes India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau, and Sri Lanka. This region is home to many ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi, and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh, and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature, and music.

CuratorialText:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms

CuratorialText: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.
persona2_adapted: Sennacherib ruled Assyria from 704 to 681 BC and is especially known for his ambitious building programs. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were adorned with a series of intricately carved stone reliefs. Many of these reliefs are now displayed in Room 9. They depict various subjects, including the transport of enormous sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu), each weighing up to 30 tons. These figures were originally placed at the main entrances of the palace. The reliefs offer valuable insight into ancient quarrying and transportation methods, as well as Sennacherib’s personal interest in his construction projects. Other panels in the room illustrate the king’s military campaigns.

CuratorialText: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.

Upon entering Room 10a, visitors encounter a pair of monumental human-headed winged bulls. These colossal figures served as supernatural guardians at the palace of King Sargon II in Khorsabad. Their purpose was to prevent all forms of evil from entering the palace.

CuratorialText: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.
persona2_adapted: In ancient Assyria, lion hunting was considered the king's sport. It symbolized the ruler's duty to protect and fight for his people. The carved stone reliefs in Room 10a show the hunting achievements of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC). These reliefs were made for his palace in Nineveh (in what is now northern Iraq). The hunting scenes are full of tension and realism. They are among the highest accomplishments of Assyrian art. The images show the lions being released, the chase that follows, and the final killing.

CuratorialText: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. CuratorialText: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

CuratorialText: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.

This was the first exhibition dedicated to Hiroshige at the British Museum, and the first in London in over twenty-five years. It offered a visually powerful portrait of a country on the verge of profound transformation.

Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) was born during a period of political and social instability in Japan. He later became one of the country’s most gifted, productive, and widely admired artists. As Japan began to face increasing pressure from the outside world, Hiroshige’s calm artistic vision connected with—and offered reassurance to—people across all levels of society.

His work captures many aspects of life in Japan during his time: fashionable figures, lively city scenes, remote landscapes, and impressions of the natural world. His bird-and-flower prints reveal a poetic sensitivity to nature, while his evocative landscapes reflect a growing fascination with travel throughout Japan. Hiroshige depicted his world sometimes as it truly was, but often as he imagined it could be.

CuratorialText: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.

Unlike most other print designers of his time, he came from a samurai family. However, he crossed social boundaries to focus on depicting popular customs. His work was also affordable. In addition to his famous landscape prints, he designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to everyone.

CuratorialText: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. CuratorialText: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. **Adapted Text:**

This head is made of brass (a type of zinc-brass with a high lead content) and was cast using the lost-wax (cire perdue) technique. It is slightly smaller than life-size and executed in a naturalistic style.

The headdress, which suggests a crown, is complex in its construction. The main tubular section of the crown wraps around the head in three layers:

- **Upper layer:** A band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, disc-shaped beads. Above these is a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel.
- **Central layer:** A row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels.
- **Lower layer:** A row of rosettes painted in red.

In addition, a projecting arc runs across the forehead. It consists of small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of this cover has eighteen vertical elements, incised to suggest braided work (plaitwork), with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides of the cover are decorated with a row of red-painted rosettes.

A crest rises from the front of the central crown. It includes a conical roundel with a central boss, surrounded by seven concentric rings—likely representing beads. Behind the roundel, a braided element rises and ends in a pointed, ovoid tip. Both elements show traces of black paint.

The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised lines (striations). Two lines of holes run between the earlobes: one passes through the angle of the neck and jaw, the other crosses the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The eyes are almond-shaped and small in proportion to the head; the brow ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated.

Around the front of the neck, grooves represent skin creases. Holes are pierced through the front and both sides of the neck. On the right side of the jaw (the viewer's right), there is a large, irregular hole.

CuratorialText: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā) solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta) held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.

**Notes on changes made:**

- **"High tubular coiffure"** → **"tall, tubular hairstyle"**: "Coiffure" is a less common word; "hairstyle" is clearer.

- **"Probably set with stones"** → **"likely set with precious stones"**: "Stones" can be ambiguous; "precious stones" is more specific.

- **"The hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring"** → **"a hand position where the tips of the fingers touch the thumb, forming a ring"**: Simplified sentence structure and vocabulary.

**Fragment of a Marble Corner Palmetto with Bird Foot**

This small fragment comes from a standing, rectangular corner palmetto that was originally attached above a volute acroterion. The palmetto has been broken off at the base.

On both sides of the central leaf—which projects outward from the corner—there are three slightly curved, hollow-shaped leaves. Their edges are outlined by a raised bar. The rounded tips of the leaves are emphasized on the outer side by small tubercles. Darts (narrow, pointed decorative elements) appear between the leaves.

On the left side of the fragment, part of a curved ovolo moulding from the volute is preserved. The upper and back surfaces show a small pentagonal block with a disc-shaped tunnel projecting at a 45-degree angle. Leaves with convex backs appear to grow out of this tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnel, two claws are visible, each with bird legs along the edges.

Traces of red paint were found on the uppermost and third darts on the left side. Additional traces of red and blue paint are also present.

The surface of the light crystalline marble is fairly smooth (sanded), but the underside is fractured.

On the right, we see the writer Daniel Defoe. A manuscript of his famous novel *Robinson Crusoe* is visible in his pocket. He is leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression. His worried wife and daughter are waiting for him.

In the foreground, there are books and a palette with paintbrushes, symbolizing the arts.

The print was made in 1851, after an original design by Ward. It uses a mixed technique: etching, aquatint, and engraving, on a type of paper called *chine collé*.

CuratorialText: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona2_adapted:
Here is the adapted version of the text, tailored for a non-native English speaker with deep art knowledge.

---

**Adapted Text:**

This Protocorinthian alabastron is a small, wheel-made vessel used for holding perfumed oil. Its body is ovoid (egg-shaped) and slightly flattened, resting on a rounded base. The mouth is small with a sloping rim, and there is a pierced lug handle at the top.

The vessel is made of fine buff clay with a slip (a thin layer of liquid clay). The painted decoration has fired black, with additional purple paint applied to the bodies of the animals. The details of the figures are incised (cut into the surface).

The main decorative band (frieze) shows two griffins (mythical creatures with the body of a lion and the head of an eagle). Between them is a stylized tree-like motif. A lion occupies the space between the two griffins. The field around the figures is filled with smaller decorative motifs (filling ornaments).

Above the main scene, there is a frieze of stylized floral designs. Below this, there are simpler motifs made of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. The vase also has simple rays (radiating lines) on its body, and tongues (tongue-shaped patterns) on the lip.

CuratorialText: IWM holds approximately 11 million photographs of modern conflict from the First World War to present day.

CuratorialText: The collection covers the activities of British and Commonwealth forces and international perspectives. Photographs from official, press, and private perspectives such as soldiers, nurses and civilians who participated in or witnessed conflict reveal the human impact of war. The collection includes notable photographers such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt and Tim Hetherington.

These images reveal the human consequences of war. The collection features work by renowned photographers including Olive Edis, Bill Brandt, and Tim Hetherington.

CuratorialText: The photographs can be viewed alongside original documents which help us understand how and why these photographs were taken.

CuratorialText:
Uniforms, Flags, Badges and Insignia

CuratorialText: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.
persona2_adapted: IWM’s Sound Collection holds more than 35,000 recordings related to conflicts since 1914. It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, containing over 60,000 hours of material and interviews in more than 15 languages. In addition to interviews, the collection includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts, and music. It documents the wartime experiences of ordinary people—such as soldiers, activists, factory workers, and journalists—as well as notable figures like Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn. This unique oral history archive has served as the foundation for significant academic research and has been widely used in bestselling books, films, television and radio programs, and podcasts. Thousands of these audio recordings are accessible on the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new content.

CuratorialText:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.

**Adapted Text:**

For over a hundred years, IWM has managed a Film Archive. From the very beginning of its existence, IWM recognized that film is an important primary source—a direct record of events. Its extensive collection shows the causes, development, and consequences of conflict, from the First World War to the present day. This is a remarkable resource for researchers and filmmakers.

Our collection continues to grow and now includes more than 25,000 hours of moving images. It covers a wide and diverse range of material: public information films, service films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and amateur films. We also hold two major collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. An ongoing film digitization program provides free online access, with newly digitized media added as soon as it becomes available.

CuratorialText:
Private Papers
IWM holds over 20,000 individual collections of private papers, principally comprising unpublished diaries, letters and memoirs written primarily by British and Commonwealth servicemen and women since 1914 and by civilians mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.
persona2_adapted:
**Private Papers**  
The Imperial War Museum (IWM) holds more than 20,000 individual collections of private papers. These consist mainly of unpublished diaries, letters, and memoirs written primarily by British and Commonwealth military personnel since 1914, as well as by civilians, especially during the two world wars. The collection also includes many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.

CuratorialText:
Foreign and Related Documents
We also look after a large collection of foreign documents, including the official British records of the major war crimes trials conducted at Nuremberg and Tokyo, together with related documents from the Second World War which mainly concern the German and Japanese war efforts.
persona2_adapted:
Foreign and Related Documents

Our collection also includes a substantial number of documents from outside the United Kingdom. Among these are the official British records of the major war crimes trials held in Nuremberg and Tokyo, along with related materials from the Second World War. These documents primarily focus on the German and Japanese war efforts.

CuratorialText: This exceptional collection is one of the most important representations of twentieth century British art in the world. It includes many great works of art from the British government war art schemes of the First and Second World Wars, which employed the greatest artists of their day, including leaders of the avant garde. These included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent and Sir William Orpen.  IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals the day-to-day running of the war art schemes and gives fascinating glimpses of the artists' experiences.

CuratorialText: Our Library collection consists of over 150,000 items covering twentieth and twenty-first century conflict involving British and Commonwealth countries. Along with material on military units and campaign histories, we have biographies, manuals, items relating to the economic, social, and cultural aspects of war, journals, newspapers, ephemera, propaganda, and e-resources.

CuratorialText: image: HMS Laurentic is seen sinking in the background, with half of the ship submerged and her bow rising up out of the water. In the foreground is a lifeboat full of sailors illuminated against the dark by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right and small dots of light in the background indicate further lifeboats. In the background on the left, the top of the German U-boat is visible. HMS Laurentic, originally a P&O liner, was equipped with 6" guns in 1939 and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. She was Worsley's first ship and he remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. Worsley painted the original immediatly afterwards, while on survivors leave. The WAAC bought the picture and in August 1942 sent it to South America in a touring exhibition of war art. On the way, however, the ship was torpedoed and the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This image shows HMS Laurentic sinking in the background. Half of the ship is underwater, and its bow rises above the surface. In the foreground, a lifeboat filled with sailors is lit by a small light against the dark water. Another lifeboat appears on the right, and small points of light in the background indicate more lifeboats. On the left side of the background, the top of a German U-boat is visible.

HMS Laurentic was originally a passenger liner for the P&O company. In 1939, it was equipped with 6-inch guns and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. This ship was Worsley’s first command, and he stayed with it until it was sunk off Foreland, Ulster, by German U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940.

Worsley painted the original version immediately after the event, while on survivor’s leave. The War Artists Advisory Committee (WAAC) purchased the painting and sent it to South America in August 1942 as part of a traveling exhibition of war art. However, during transport, the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed. The present work was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC.

*(Source: Exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)*

CuratorialText: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' [Eric Rimmington, from the exhibition catalogue 'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing, A Brewery Arts Centre Touring Exhibition' 1987.] The artist made this series of drawings at a time of intense anti-nuclear protests against both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. The 1979 decision by the British Government to allow American Cruise Missiles to be sited in the UK sparked a series of marches and protests. These included the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, started in 1981 and which was at its height in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. The accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, also in 1979, heightened fears about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste and its transport and storage continues to be a focus of protest and anxiety. The Greater London Assembly commissioned a report into the safety of nuclear waste trains in 2001 for this reason.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

*Image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall beside a railway cutting. The wall has graffiti reading: 'Nuclear state = Police state', followed by an anarchy symbol, and then 'One World'. Behind the wall on the right is a large industrial building. Further right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.*

In the summer of 1983, artist Eric Rimmington discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove. This led him to create a series of ten drawings of the railway line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. While working on the second drawing, he realized that this same line was used several times a week to transport nuclear waste from Sizewell nuclear power station to Sellafield, a nuclear fuel reprocessing plant. Over the next six weeks, he found it impossible to ignore this fact, and it became deeply connected to every mark he made. (Rimmington, from the 1987 exhibition catalogue *'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing'*)

The artist created this series during a period of intense anti-nuclear protests, which targeted both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. In 1979, the British government decided to allow American Cruise Missiles to be stationed in the UK. This decision sparked a series of marches and protests, including the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, which began in 1981 and reached its peak in 1983, when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. That same year, the accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, increased public fears about the safety of nuclear power.

The transport and storage of nuclear waste remain a source of protest and concern. For this reason, in 2001, the Greater London Assembly commissioned a report on the safety of nuclear waste trains.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Five children are seated around a table in room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the centre with the youngest child on her lap. A large 'Spotted-Dick' pudding is placed on the table with several bowls and cutlery set around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right who holds out a bowl for his portion.
Label
'Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings.' (title from folder cover, Children in Wartime).
persona2_adapted:
**Object Description**  
The image shows five children seated around a table in a room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the center, holding the youngest child on her lap. On the table, there is a large "Spotted Dick" pudding (a traditional British steamed suet pudding with dried fruit), along with several bowls and utensils. The woman is serving a boy on the far right, who holds out a bowl to receive his portion.

**Label**  
"Mrs. Norris, from Cookham, is pictured with five of the seven children from London who were part of her 'war family.' By January 1941, these children had been living with her for sixteen months. Mrs. Norris represents the ideal foster parent, and many other householders like her existed at the time. Over a million children had been moved from evacuation areas in organized school groups. Given such large numbers, placing children in private homes (billets) was the main way to provide them with accommodation. Thanks to Mrs. Norris’s warmth and care, city children quickly adapted to their new surroundings." (Title from folder cover: *Children in Wartime*)

**Notes for the reader:**  
- *Spotted Dick*: a traditional English pudding; the name may sound unusual but refers to a dessert with dried fruit ("spots").  
- *Billets*: private homes where evacuees were temporarily housed.  
- *Evacuation areas*: cities considered at risk of bombing during World War II, from which children were sent to safer rural locations.  
- The cultural context here is the British wartime evacuation program (1939–1945), which moved children from urban areas to the countryside for safety.

CuratorialText: image: portrait of a female factory worker operating a lathe. Miss Loftus had been brought to the attention of the War Artist's Advisory Committee as 'an outstanding factory worker'. Knight expected to do a studio portrait but the Ministry of Supply requested that she be painted at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. Making a Bofors Breech ring was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years training. Loftus was aged 21 at the time of the painting and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine presented a considerable publicity coup at the time. However, it has been suggested that she was placed at the machine precisely for this reason. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment. Loftus was an outstanding factory worker who had mastered complex engineering skills in a very short space of time, and Knight was commissioned to paint her at work in the factory. Knight was fascinated by circus artistes and dancers, and she emphasises the balance and posture of her subject at work. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This portrait shows a female factory worker operating a lathe. Miss Loftus was brought to the attention of the War Artist's Advisory Committee because she was described as "an outstanding factory worker." The artist, Knight, originally planned to paint a studio portrait, but the Ministry of Supply requested that Loftus be shown at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. Making a Bofors Breech ring was considered the most skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years of training. At the time of the painting, Loftus was 21 years old and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine was a significant public relations success for the war effort. However, some have suggested that she was placed at the machine precisely for this reason—to create a powerful image.

Industrial machinery was a completely new subject for Knight, but contemporary reports praised her technical accuracy. Like Loftus, Knight was proving herself in a traditionally male environment. Knight was fascinated by circus performers and dancers, and in this painting she emphasizes the balance and posture of her subject while working.

CuratorialText:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

On the night of October 23, 1943, two British destroyers—HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—were sailing along the Turkish coast toward Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands. At that time, Leros was occupied by a British garrison. On board the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations for the Middle East. He was traveling to inspect the island’s defenses. The ships also carried troops, ammunition, and supplies for the garrison.

At 11:45 p.m., the *Eclipse* struck a naval mine between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The explosion caused the ship to break apart and sink in only two minutes. In addition to its regular crew of about 200 men, the ship was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company from the Buffs (a British infantry regiment). Only about 100 men survived. They were rescued by an air-sea rescue launch sent from Leros and by boats from HMS *Petard*, after spending more than three hours in the water.

Brigadier-General Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it happened.

**Notes for the reader:**
- *The Buffs*: A historical nickname for the Royal East Kent Regiment, a British infantry unit.
- *Air-sea rescue launch*: A small, fast boat used to rescue people from the water.
- *Garrison*: A group of soldiers stationed in a specific location for defense.

CuratorialText: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

Recognized as one of the greatest landscape painters in history, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford very well. His famous oil painting of Oxford’s High Street is currently on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery.

The collection includes lively studies of animals, birds, and fish, along with evocative scenes of places the artist visited during his travels in Britain and Europe—from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.
persona2_adapted: During the second millennium BCE, the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as the Minoans. In 1900, when archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans began excavating the site of Knossos on Crete, he often linked the objects he discovered to ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the "Palace of Minos," after the legendary king of Crete, and associated it with the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an intricate maze that King Minos ordered to be built in order to imprison the fierce Minotaur—a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. According to the myth, the Minotaur was killed by Theseus, a famous semi-divine hero. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece, became king, and was regarded as the founder of Athens. These Cretan myths originated from stories that the Greek and Roman inhabitants of Knossos would have told long after the palace had been destroyed.

CuratorialText:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.
persona2_adapted:
Landscape painting has a long history in East Asian art. However, it was not until the early 1800s that landscape became a major subject in Japanese woodblock prints. At that time, Japanese people began to participate more in outdoor leisure activities, such as visiting temples and taking boat trips. This created a growing demand for souvenir images. In the highly competitive publishing industry, publishers constantly sought new products, and landscape prints offered an attractive novelty.

Technical developments also played a key role in the rise of landscape prints. One especially important factor was the introduction of a new imported pigment: Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded quickly, this brilliant color gave artists greater freedom of expression, particularly in depicting sky and water.

In this section, we present a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.

In addition, the museum holds one of the oldest, largest, and best-preserved collections of plaster casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the United Kingdom.

CuratorialText: The Western Art collections contain outstanding European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to the present day. They include Old Master and later paintings, drawings, watercolours, and prints; English and continental ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's exceptional print collection contains around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints and many are accessible through our Western Art Print Room.

CuratorialText: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.

**Adapted Text:**

This painting originated from an important early commission for two watercolours. These were intended to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, known as the *Oxford Almanack*. The watercolours were so successful that Turner eventually completed ten of them between 1798 and 1804. Later, this work and one other were painted in oils for James Wyatt, a local print-seller and frame-maker. Turner consulted Wyatt about the architectural details included in the view. He also added figures—members of the University and clergy—as well as some women, “for the sake of colour.”

This painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works. The reason is that Wyatt kept the correspondence he had with the artist, and these letters were included in Wyatt’s posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. However, Wyatt decided on an oil painting instead of a watercolour. The canvas was half the size of Turner’s usual works, and the cost was 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812.

In the 1830s, when Turner was selecting views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he chose not to include the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved a level of technical mastery that was unmatched and could not be repeated.

CuratorialText: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.
persona2_adapted:
Here is the adapted version of the text, tailored for a non-native English speaker with deep art knowledge. The vocabulary has been simplified where possible, idioms and cultural references have been clarified, and the sentence structure has been made more direct—while preserving all the original art-historical information.

---

**Adapted Text:**

*The Hunt in the Forest* is a highly original painting. It is a nocturnal landscape and a brilliantly structured composition. Hunting was an aristocratic activity with its own rituals. However, the idea of a night hunt is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic.

To prepare this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first drew a grid on the surface of the panel. He fixed a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. When painting the subject, he included specific details: the huntsmen’s spears, cut branches, logs, and the water in the foreground. These elements serve as devices to focus attention on this space and on the receding forms of men and animals.

Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colors of the figures against the dark forest. When illuminated by candlelight, these gold flecks would have glistened and appeared to move.

Paolo di Dono (1397–1475) was celebrated during his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals, and landscape. His nickname, Uccello (which means “Bird” in Italian), refers to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer and sometimes worked on mosaic and stained-glass commissions. All of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece.

*The Hunt* is what is known as a *spalliera* painting. This type of painting would have been mounted at shoulder height—either as the backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace. It may have been made for a palace in Urbino, where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence around 1470.

CuratorialText: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. CuratorialText: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.
persona2_adapted:
Three of the objects in this still life also appear in other late works by Renoir:

- The silver beaker is shown with a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life, said to have been painted in Cagnes in 1910. It appears again in a similar composition without the knife, also dated to 1910.
- The bronze-colored child figure on the column appears in a small study dated around 1905 by the Daubervilles.
- The faience double salt container—a type widely produced in the 19th century at Desvres (Pas-de-Calais) and Quimper (Brittany)—appears in at least four other sketches.

A floating, bodiless image of the same figure, seen from behind, appears in the upper right area. This is probably a reflection in a mirror.

Renoir began his career painting at a ceramics factory. He seems to have owned several pieces of this popular type of faience,⁵ and he used a variety of colored and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.

CuratorialText: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
persona2_adapted: This panel originally formed part of the predella of a large altarpiece created between 1433 and 1435 for the church of San Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence. The altarpiece is now disassembled, with its various sections held in different collections. The central panel, depicting the Virgin and Child with Four Angels, was flanked by pairs of saints: Saint Benedict and Saint Nicholas of Bari on one side, and Saint John the Baptist and an Evangelist (likely Saint Matthew) on the other. The predella illustrated scenes from the life of Saint Nicholas, of which five are known today: the saint's birth; pilgrims at his shrine; his revival of three youths; his provision of dowries for three sisters (symbolized by three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and his calming of a storm. The panels likely originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the composition of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altarpiece of 1425, created for the church of San Niccolò Oltrarno.

CuratorialText: The pages of calligraphy, all by one hand, mostly include kanshi (Chinese poems), and are signed with variations of the name Sessō Keigi. If the calligraphy was done at the same time as the paintings, this album would be an early work by Sobun before he began participating in the national and international expositions.

This work caused him to be questioned by the Inquisition, who accused it of being blasphemous. To avoid punishment, Veronese changed the title of the painting to *The Feast in the House of Levi*. CuratorialText: The painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse, with its distinctive eighteenth-century château and garden terrace on the left and the mountains of the Petit Luberon in the background. Guigou was seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks interspersed with the channels of the river Durance as it passes between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron during the dry season. As in most of the artist’s Provençal landscapes, the air is clear and the sunlight strong. The example of Corot or Daubigny may have had some influence on the choice of the panoramic view that Guigou seems to have favoured above all others, but his manner of painting is distinctive. The sky, sand and river are thinly brushed and enlivened with spots of impasto and small, short strokes with the tip of the brush in the artist’s characteristic manner.

CuratorialText: When Pharaoh ordered all new-born Hebrew sons to be killed, Moses's mother put him in an ark of bulrushes in the Nile. His sister stayed to watch while Pharaoh's daughter came to the river with her maidens and found the child (Exodus 2: 2-4). WA1950.169 is striking for its emotional intensity, with the focus on Moses's grief-stricken parents. The joyful scene of the finding of Moses was a popular one, whereas this subject is far less common (Poussin earlier treated it c. 1627). Painted in 1654 for Jacques Stella, WA1950.169 was greatly admired by contemporaries for the artist's learned treatment of the subject, his skill in painting reflections and the beauty of the landscape. Pharoah's city is based on Pirro Ligorio's reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx accompanied by a river-god and cornucopia identifies the Nile. Different explanations, based on Poussin's antiquarian interests and wide-ranging visual references, have been proposed for the significance of the pipes of Pan that hang from a tree behind this group. Two painted copies are recorded in the 17th century; other copies and variations are known (including a tapestry version of the right side) and three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella's niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.

When the Pharaoh ordered the killing of all newborn Hebrew boys, Moses’s mother placed him in a basket made of bulrushes (a type of reed) and set it in the Nile River. The painting WA1950.169 is notable for its strong emotional impact. Unlike the more common joyful scene of Moses being found, this work focuses on the grief of Moses’s parents. The artist, Nicolas Poussin, had already treated the joyful version around 1627. This painting, created in 1654 for Jacques Stella, was highly admired by Poussin’s contemporaries for its learned approach to the subject, the artist’s skill in painting reflections, and the beauty of the landscape.

The city of Pharaoh is based on Pirro Ligorio’s reconstruction of famous buildings from ancient Rome. A sphinx, together with a river god and a cornucopia (a symbol of abundance), identifies the Nile. Behind this group, a set of Pan pipes hangs from a tree. Scholars have proposed different explanations for their meaning, based on Poussin’s interest in antiquities and his use of many visual references.

Two painted copies from the 17th century are known, along with other copies and variations (including a tapestry version of the right side). Three engraved versions were also made, the first by Stella’s niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.

CuratorialText: Design is an impulse that unifies us across time and culture. This installation unifies two traditionally disparate collecting fields, fashion and textiles with decorative and industrial arts to better understand what underlies our motivations and capacity for design. Ensembles from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the exuberant remixing and inventive styling of one of the world’s most prominent fashion icons, while constellations of unique and culturally significant works of design, fashion, and textiles explore distinctive and resourceful forms of creative expression. A recent gallery highlight is the display of 38 vibrant and flamboyant ensembles from the collection of Boston-based entrepreneur and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Yolanda’s, based in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a hotspot for lavish evening and wedding ensembles and that became a household name throughout the region, and a shorthand for glitz and glamor in the late 20th century. Cellucci generously donated 57 works of fashion and accessories to PEM in 2021, along with archival photographs and materials related to the legacy of her bridal shop.

CuratorialText: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to achieve international recognition. Beginning her career in Boston in 1863, she traveled to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her generation, breaking international, racial and gender barriers. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way,” Lewis recalled in 1878. “That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.” Following her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy endured in Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely been underrecognized. Some of her great masterpieces were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her vivid, naturalistic stone sculptures feature abolitionists and social reformers, engage with Indigenous identity and give new life to religious and mythological figures.

CuratorialText: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest spans nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle, and makes up one of the world’s last remaining stretches of true wilderness. Discover the biodiversity and global importance of our northernmost forests through first-person stories, commissioned objects, interactive experiences and exquisite photography and videography.
